Strong utility accountability also makes each water-allocation trade-off visible to the communities, firms and ecosystems affected by it.

Continue to model essays

Idea-building model

5. Advanced C2 essay

Question: Can a circular economy remain credible if total consumption continues to rise?
Extended model · 1705 words · designed to build arguments, not imitate exam length

The circular economy promises to replace the linear sequence of extraction, production and disposal with systems that preserve value. Products are repaired, materials recycled, wastewater reused and organic matter converted into energy or nutrients. The model is attractive because it appears to reconcile environmental limits with continuing prosperity. Yet its credibility depends on a difficult question: can circularity compensate for rising total consumption?

What makes this question unavoidable is the difference between relative efficiency and absolute material use. A product may contain more recycled content and require fewer resources per unit, while the economy produces so many additional units that the total material footprint still grows. Circular improvement at the product level does not automatically create sustainability at the system level. Recycling illustrates the limitation. Collection and material recovery can return metals, paper and plastics to production, but recovery is incomplete. Materials are lost through contamination, export, combustion and technical degradation. Some products combine substances that are difficult to separate. Even perfect collection would not eliminate demand for primary resources when the economy expands.

Time creates another constraint. Materials remain inside buildings, vehicles and infrastructure for years. A rapidly growing economy needs inputs today, while much of its existing material stock will become available for recycling only in the future. Secondary supply therefore cannot match unlimited immediate growth.

Energy also matters. Sorting, cleaning and remanufacturing require transport and power. Recycling usually saves energy compared with primary extraction, but not always by the same amount. Lifecycle assessment is necessary to identify when a supposed circular solution merely moves emissions or pollution between stages.

Water systems show both the promise and limits of circularity. Water reuse can reduce freshwater abstraction, while treatment plants recover energy and nutrients. However, every treatment step consumes infrastructure, chemicals and electricity. Reuse does not make water demand irrelevant. A city that continually expands consumption may still exceed its catchment even after recycling part of the flow.

Were efficiency gains sufficient by themselves, decades of technological improvement would already have produced a sustained decline in global resource use. Instead, lower costs and greater convenience can stimulate additional demand. This rebound effect is not inevitable, but it demonstrates why policy cannot rely only on better technology. The most credible circular framework therefore begins with the waste hierarchy. Prevention and reduced demand come before recycling. Product durability, repair and shared use slow the flow of materials through the economy. A washing machine used for fifteen years requires fewer replacements than one recycled after five, even if both eventually enter a recovery facility.

Business models may support this shift. Leasing can give manufacturers an incentive to maintain products and recover components. Producer responsibility can require companies to take back goods after use. Repairability rules prevent firms from using design or software to force premature replacement. Yet these policies challenge revenue models based on frequent sales.

This is where circularity becomes political rather than merely technical. Governments may celebrate recycling while avoiding policies that reduce consumption because those policies affect growth, advertising and corporate profits. A deposit system is easier to introduce than a serious debate about how many disposable containers should exist. Commercial incentives can also select the easiest materials. High-value metals attract recovery, while mixed plastics or contaminated textiles remain difficult. Investors favour projects with predictable revenue, not necessarily those producing the greatest environmental benefit. Public rules must therefore define priorities rather than assuming markets will close every loop.

The language of circularity can also support greenwashing. A company may advertise recyclable packaging without showing whether collection infrastructure exists. Another may use a small amount of recycled material while increasing total production. Metrics should report absolute virgin-material use, product lifespan and recovery rates, not only isolated success stories.

Many companies have adopted circular language, yet total waste generation has continued to rise. This does not prove the concept is useless. It shows that implementation has focused more heavily on downstream management than upstream prevention. Social justice adds another dimension. Repair, reuse and recycling industries can create jobs, but they may also rely on informal labour exposed to toxic materials. Waste exports can transfer environmental harm to countries with weaker regulation. A circular economy that preserves materials while sacrificing workers would solve the wrong problem.

Universal services are essential. In communities without reliable waste collection or safe wastewater treatment, sophisticated circular markets are premature. Public investment must first prevent open dumping, sewage exposure and contaminated water. Circular innovation should strengthen basic systems rather than divert attention from them.

Affordability also matters. Durable products may cost more upfront, even if they save money over time. Deposits and tariffs can burden households with limited cash. Policy needs targeted support, repair vouchers or public services so circularity does not become a premium lifestyle for wealthy consumers.

The polluter-pays principle provides one answer. Producers and users should face costs that reflect waste and pollution, while revenue supports transition and low-income households. However, firms may feed costs through to consumers through higher prices. Distributional analysis is therefore necessary; environmental pricing is not automatically fair merely because the principle sounds fair.

Water tariffs reveal the same tension. Higher prices encourage conservation, but drinking water is essential. A progressive tariff can protect basic consumption while charging more for excessive use. Utilities must also repair leaks and disclose performance. Asking households to conserve while large volumes disappear from pipes undermines public confidence.

Circularity also requires durable institutions. Product standards, material registries, treatment monitoring and cross-border rules demand institutional capacity. A country may adopt ambitious legislation but lack inspectors, laboratories or collection vehicles. The implementation gap can turn circular targets into ceremonial policy. Only when governments measure absolute resource use alongside circular activity can progress be judged honestly. Recycling rates, repair markets and reuse volumes remain useful indicators, but they should be connected to extraction, consumption and pollution.

There are sectors where growth and circularity can coexist more easily. Digital services may create value with less material than heavy construction, although data centres still consume energy and equipment. Health, education, maintenance and cultural activity can expand without the same resource intensity as disposable goods. Economic development does not require every source of revenue to involve greater physical throughput.

Urban planning can also reduce demand structurally. Compact cities require less road, pipe and vehicle infrastructure per resident. Shared mobility, adaptable buildings and public laundries can provide services with fewer assets. These changes depend on design and institutions, not on continuous consumer self-denial. Had products and infrastructure been designed for disassembly and reuse from the beginning, current recovery systems might face fewer technical barriers. Legacy systems create today’s waste, but new design determines future options.

A credible circular economy must therefore make two promises. It should recover much more value from the resources already used, and it should reduce the rate at which new resources enter the system. The first promise without the second risks becoming efficient disposal inside an expanding linear economy. Not only must circular policy close material loops, but it must also slow the total flow through them. This does not require identical consumption limits for every society. Lower-income regions still need infrastructure and essential goods, while high-consuming groups have greater capacity to reduce wasteful demand.

Water reform needs evidence-based policymaking and transparent cost-benefit analysis, yet both should protect long-term public value. Equitable access requires affordable basic supply for households and reliable service for essential workers who keep cities functioning.

Utilities depend on skilled human capital. Lifelong learning and transferable skills help technicians manage reuse, leakage and recovery systems. Targeted support can make these careers a route to intergenerational mobility rather than a narrow technical niche.

Unreliable drinking water creates chronic stress and damages mental wellbeing. Even households with secure employment struggle when contamination or price shocks intensify. Policy must remove structural barriers that leave informal settlements outside safe networks.

Restrictions should reflect individual circumstances and a clear evidence threshold. Vulnerable users need legal safeguards, while hiring and training should remove employment barriers. Transparent decisions can rebuild public confidence when drought requires difficult allocation.

Smart meters need algorithmic transparency because automated alerts can deepen information asymmetry. Effective regulatory oversight should preserve procedural fairness and protect freedom of expression when residents challenge a disputed bill or report pollution.

Water data should follow data minimisation and serve a legitimate purpose. Independent oversight can close an accountability gap between utilities, contractors and regulators. Institutions must also build up trusted capacity to act on the evidence.

Repair, collection and treatment can create entry-level roles, but automation should support worker augmentation rather than abrupt job displacement. Public contracts can provide paid training and share productivity gains with the workers making circular systems function.

Innovation requires funding continuity and scientific independence. Mission-driven research can improve membranes or nutrient recovery, while replication studies reveal whether pilots work elsewhere. Open knowledge spillovers prevent public evidence from becoming private advertising.

Catchment planning combines Earth observation and satellite data with field sampling. Long-term climate monitoring and reliable weather forecasting reveal changing supply, while a coordinated disaster response protects water quality after floods, fires or infrastructure failure.

Water security is central to climate adaptation. Stable adaptation finance can strengthen flood resilience and early-warning systems. Where repeated drought or contamination makes service untenable, carefully planned managed retreat may become part of an honest strategy.

Circular policy should not accelerate biodiversity loss. Healthy ecosystem services, soil biodiversity and wetlands protect water quality. Nature-positive development can also slow pollinator decline by restoring habitat rather than treating land only as infrastructure.

Drought threatens food security, while discarded food wastes embedded water. Governments must confront market concentration in disposal and treatment, strengthen resilient supply chains, respond directly to water scarcity and prevent food waste before promising recovery.

Finally, polluted services can intensify housing insecurity. A credible land-use trade-off should protect wetlands as well as sites for treatment. Strong municipal delivery capacity makes sustainable urban development practical, while poor maintenance can add to costs and contamination.

Circularity remains credible if it is treated as a framework for sufficiency, durability and recovery rather than as permission for endless growth in physical consumption. The economy can create value through better services, maintenance and knowledge. But no loop is perfect, and a planet with finite materials cannot recycle its way out of infinite throughput.

Exam-length model

6. Realistic IELTS essay · approximately 300 words

Question: Some people believe recycling is the most important solution to waste problems. Others argue that governments should focus on reducing consumption and making products last longer. Discuss both views and give your opinion.
Model answer · 301 words

Recycling is the most visible part of modern waste policy, and many people regard it as the main solution to growing rubbish. Others argue that prevention, reuse and durability deserve greater attention. In my view, recycling is necessary, but reducing material demand should remain the priority.

Supporters of recycling point out that it recovers useful materials and reduces pressure on landfill sites. Separate collection can turn paper, metal and plastic into new industrial inputs. Requirements for recycled content also create markets for these materials. What recycling provides is a practical route for products that have already reached the end of use. However, recovery is incomplete. Mixed or contaminated materials are difficult to process, and some products are recyclable only in theory. Recycling also consumes energy and cannot preserve all material quality. Only when products are designed for separation can recycling operate efficiently at scale.

Prevention preserves more value. Governments can shift away from unnecessary single-use plastics, introduce repairability standards and require producers to take back equipment. Longer product durability reduces the number of replacements manufactured in the first place. Recycling systems have expanded in many countries, yet total municipal waste has continued to grow.

Policy should follow the waste hierarchy. Recycling facilities and universal collection remain essential, but taxes, procurement and design standards should encourage reuse and repair. Deposit schemes can support both returnable packaging and high-quality recycling. The social effects also need attention. Higher product standards may increase upfront prices, so low-income households need access to affordable repair and durable goods. Had products been designed for repair earlier, many functioning devices might not have become electronic waste.

In conclusion, recycling is an important final defence against disposal, but it cannot compensate for unlimited material consumption. Governments should reduce unnecessary products, extend useful life and then recycle the remaining materials effectively.

Why the exam-length essay is strong

Direct position

The introduction recognises recycling while giving prevention and product durability a higher priority.

Causal explanation

The essay connects product design, consumption, collection quality and markets for recovered materials.

Developed contrast

Downstream recycling is weighed against upstream measures that stop waste from being created.

Policy mechanism

Repair standards, producer responsibility, deposit returns and recycled-content rules make the position practical.

Recycled language

Earlier collocations return as part of the reasoning rather than as decoration.

Controlled complexity

Advanced grammar remains clear enough for realistic exam conditions.

7. Advanced grammar transformations

1. If cities repaired leaks earlier, fewer households would now face restrictions. (Past-perfect conditional)

Had cities repaired leaks earlier, fewer households would now face restrictions.

2. Utilities will gain trust only when monitoring is transparent. (Negative inversion)

Only when monitoring is transparent will utilities gain trust.

3. Waste prevention matters most in a circular economy. (Cleft sentence)

What matters most in a circular economy is waste prevention.

4. Governments should reduce demand and expand reuse. (Balanced recommendation)

Governments should both reduce demand and expand reuse.

5. The product was designed for convenience, but it became difficult to recycle. (Participle clause)

Designed for convenience, the product became difficult to recycle.

6. Although desalination is reliable, it remains energy-intensive. (Fronted concession)

Reliable though desalination may be, it remains energy-intensive.

7. Treatment plants recover water and generate energy. (Not only...but also)

Not only do treatment plants recover water, but they also generate energy.

8. Countries have expanded recycling, but total waste has continued to rise. (Present-perfect contrast)

Countries have expanded recycling, yet total waste has continued to rise.

9. The company closed the dump after contamination had reached groundwater. (Past perfect)

The company closed the dump after contamination had reached groundwater.

10. The system lacks enforcement, so illegal dumping continues. (Nominalisation)

The system’s lack of enforcement allows illegal dumping to continue.

11. If products lasted longer, material demand would fall. (Conditional inversion)

Material demand would fall were products to last longer.

12. Residents rejected the project because officials avoided consultation. (Cleft cause)

It was the absence of community water consultation that caused residents to reject the project.

13. Cities should collect waste, protect workers and create material markets. (Controlled parallelism)

Cities should collect waste, protect workers and create material markets.

14. The authority introduced tariffs gradually, so households could adapt. (Participle clause)

Introducing tariffs gradually, the authority gave households time to adapt.

15. Engineers changed the treatment process after new evidence appeared. (Emphatic do)

Engineers did change the treatment process after new evidence appeared.

16. No issue matters more than safe drinking water. (Negative inversion)

Rarely does any issue matter more than safe drinking water.

17. If recycled materials had stable buyers, more facilities would invest. (Conditional inversion)

More facilities would invest were recycled materials to have stable buyers.

18. The policy should be efficient, fair and enforceable. (Controlled parallelism)

The policy should improve efficiency, preserve fairness and ensure enforcement.

8. Native Academic Toolbox

1. Upgrade: “The city is running out of water.” using water scarcity.

The city faces structural water scarcity rather than a temporary inconvenience.

2. Upgrade: “A lot of water disappears from pipes.” using non-revenue water.

High non-revenue water indicates major physical and commercial losses.

3. Upgrade: “The factory uses treated water again.” using industrial reuse.

Industrial reuse reduces demand for high-quality drinking-water supplies.

4. Upgrade: “The city makes drinking water from the sea.” using reverse osmosis.

Reverse osmosis allows coastal cities to produce drinking water from seawater.

5. Upgrade: “The treatment plant makes useful products.” using resource recovery.

Resource recovery converts treatment residues into water, nutrients and energy.

6. Upgrade: “Products should be easier to fix.” using repairability standards.

Repairability standards can extend product life and reduce premature disposal.

7. Upgrade: “Companies should collect their old products.” using producer responsibility.

Producer responsibility requires firms to finance or organise end-of-life management.

8. Upgrade: “The recycling system needs buyers.” using recycled content.

Recycled-content requirements create stable demand for secondary materials.

9. Upgrade: “The city burns too much waste.” using waste hierarchy.

The waste hierarchy places prevention and recycling above energy recovery.

10. Upgrade: “Polluters should pay for the damage.” using polluter-pays principle.

The polluter-pays principle assigns clean-up costs to those creating pollution.

11. Upgrade: “Water policy needs several connected solutions.” using systems thinking.

Systems thinking connects demand, treatment, ecosystems and social access.

12. Upgrade: “The policy exists but does not work.” using implementation gap.

The implementation gap remains visible in illegal dumping and unreliable collection.

13. Upgrade: “The product uses too many new resources.” using material footprint.

The product’s material footprint includes extraction across its full supply chain.

14. Upgrade: “Water reuse needs public acceptance.” using public confidence.

Public confidence depends on independent monitoring and transparent communication.

15. Upgrade: “Waste facilities affect poor neighbourhoods more.” using distributional effects.

The distributional effects of waste infrastructure often fall on politically weaker communities.

9. IELTS Speaking

Part 1 · 15 questions

PART 1 · 1

Do you try to save water at home?

Suggested phrasal verbs
dial backput into operation
Model answer · 87 words

I try to dial back unnecessary water use by taking shorter showers, repairing dripping taps and running full loads of laundry. Personal habits help, although utilities must also put better metering into operation and fix leaks in the network. I do not think household conservation should become an excuse for ignoring industrial demand or ageing pipes. The most effective approach combines sensible behaviour with infrastructure that makes waste visible and preventable. I also check consumption over time rather than assuming one good habit cancels several wasteful ones.

PART 1 · 2

Would you drink recycled water?

Suggested phrasal verbs
turn intodesign in
Model answer · 80 words

I would drink properly treated recycled water if independent monitoring showed that it met the same safety standard as ordinary supply. Advanced treatment can turn wastewater into a reliable source, although the process needs transparent testing. Utilities should design in several barriers against contamination and explain them clearly. Public acceptance depends less on the original source than on whether institutions communicate honestly and respond quickly when results change. Public agencies should publish results in a form ordinary residents can understand.

PART 1 · 3

Do water bills affect your behaviour?

Suggested phrasal verbs
feed costs throughdial back
Model answer · 86 words

Water bills influence behaviour only when charges are understandable and households can control consumption. Utilities may feed infrastructure costs through to customers, but tariffs should protect basic use and avoid punishing large families or low-income residents. Higher prices can encourage people to dial back waste, yet metering and leak repair must come first. Charging someone for water lost inside an old building would be an impressive but unfair form of conservation policy. Social tariffs can protect essential use while preserving an incentive to reduce luxury demand.

PART 1 · 4

Have you experienced water restrictions?

Suggested phrasal verbs
run outuse up
Model answer · 85 words

I have experienced temporary restrictions during dry weather. They usually limit garden watering or other non-essential use before reservoirs run out. The measures make sense, but authorities should introduce them early rather than after a region has almost used up its reserves. Clear explanations matter because people cooperate more readily when they understand the shortage, the duration and what major users are doing alongside households. Large commercial users should face visible targets so restrictions do not appear symbolic.

Early communication also reduces confusion and panic.

PART 1 · 5

Do you recycle household waste?

Suggested phrasal verbs
sort outdispose of
Model answer · 84 words

I separate common materials when the local system is clear. The difficulty is that different labels and collection rules make it hard to sort out what belongs in each container. People may dispose of recyclable items because they fear contaminating a whole load. Standardised instructions and feedback would help. Recycling is useful, but avoiding unnecessary packaging and keeping products longer should come before placing everything in a brightly coloured bin. Deposit and refill systems are often simpler than asking people to interpret complicated packaging.

PART 1 · 6

Would you repair an old appliance?

Suggested phrasal verbs
dispose ofdesign in
Model answer · 82 words

I would repair an appliance if the cost was reasonable and spare parts were available. Manufacturers should design in repairability instead of sealing components or ending software support early. At present, people often dispose of devices because diagnosis and repair cost almost as much as replacement. Clear repair scores and longer warranties could change that calculation. Durability should be treated as normal product quality rather than a niche environmental feature. Independent repair shops also need access to manuals, tools and replacement components.

PART 1 · 7

Do you use reusable containers?

Suggested phrasal verbs
shift away fromdial back
Model answer · 79 words

I use reusable bottles and shopping bags because they are simple ways to dial back disposable packaging. Governments should shift away from the most unnecessary single-use plastics, but reusable systems need washing, collection and convenient return points. A heavy container used only twice may not perform well environmentally. The useful question is how many times it is reused and whether the whole system makes reuse easier than disposal. Reuse becomes normal only when return is as convenient as purchase.

PART 1 · 8

Would you buy second-hand products?

Suggested phrasal verbs
take backdispose of
Model answer · 84 words

I would buy second-hand furniture, books and electronics when condition and safety are clear. Retailers could take back used products, test them and offer warranties. This would reduce the number of usable items people dispose of. Trust is the main issue because buyers need accurate information about repairs, batteries and missing parts. A professional resale market can make reuse convenient rather than requiring every consumer to become an amateur technician. Refurbished products should include clear warranties so lower price does not mean hidden risk.

PART 1 · 9

Is tap water safe where you live?

Suggested phrasal verbs
clean upfeed costs through
Model answer · 85 words

Tap water is generally safe, although confidence depends on regular testing and open reporting. When pollution occurs, the responsible organisation should pay to clean up the source instead of allowing treatment costs to be fed through permanently to households. Safe water begins with protecting rivers and groundwater, not merely adding more chemicals at the treatment plant. Consumers should also receive prompt advice during any temporary failure. Catchment protection can also reduce the amount of treatment required.

Independent testing also helps residents distinguish evidence from rumour.

PART 1 · 10

Do you avoid bottled water?

Suggested phrasal verbs
shift away fromdial back
Model answer · 81 words

I avoid bottled water when the tap supply is safe and convenient. Public fountains and refill stations help people dial back packaging without carrying several litres from home. Cities could shift away from unnecessary bottled water in public buildings, although emergency and travel situations still require flexibility. The environmental benefit depends on reliable drinking-water infrastructure; people cannot be lectured about plastic while public taps are broken or unavailable. Public fountains should be maintained, not installed for one publicity photograph and forgotten.

PART 1 · 11

Would you pay a deposit on bottles?

Suggested phrasal verbs
take backsort out
Model answer · 82 words

I would pay a small deposit because it creates a clear reason to return packaging. Shops can take back containers, while automated systems sort out different materials and brands. Deposit schemes work best when return points are convenient and the refund is large enough to matter. They also improve material quality because bottles are collected separately. The system should include small retailers without placing an unreasonable burden on them. Digital refunds should remain optional so people without banking apps are not excluded.

PART 1 · 12

Do you think your city collects waste well?

Suggested phrasal verbs
put into operationclean up
Model answer · 80 words

Collection is reasonably regular, but recycling rules and bulky-waste services could improve. Cities should put consistent collection into operation before blaming residents for poor results. They also need teams to clean up illegal dumping quickly, because neglected sites attract more waste. Good collection is basic public infrastructure: it protects streets, workers and waterways. Digital apps may help, but they cannot replace vehicles, staff and treatment capacity. Collection workers need safe conditions because circularity is not achieved by hiding labour risk.

PART 1 · 13

Would you use less water during a drought?

Suggested phrasal verbs
dial backrun out
Model answer · 80 words

I would dial back non-essential use during a drought, especially outdoor watering and long showers. Restrictions are easier to accept when authorities explain how close reservoirs are to running out and whether businesses face similar rules. It would be frustrating if households saved water while large leaks continued. Conservation works best when people can see that responsibility is shared and infrastructure is being repaired. Water-saving advice should distinguish meaningful changes from tiny gestures.

Clear targets can make cooperation feel worthwhile.

PART 1 · 14

Do you compost food waste?

Suggested phrasal verbs
break apartturn into
Model answer · 79 words

I compost some plant-based scraps because microorganisms break apart the material and turn it into a useful soil amendment. The process reduces the amount sent to landfill, but it requires space and reasonable separation. Municipal collection is important for apartments and cooked food that home systems cannot manage easily. Prevention still matters more: composting an uneaten meal does not recover the water, energy and labour used to produce it. Cities can also recover energy from separately collected organic material.

PART 1 · 15

Would you live near a recycling facility?

Suggested phrasal verbs
close downsort out
Model answer · 86 words

I would live near a well-regulated facility if noise, traffic, fire risk and air quality were managed transparently. Authorities should close down unsafe operations, not assume that environmental services can impose unlimited burdens on nearby residents. Good design can sort out materials efficiently while protecting workers and neighbours. Communities hosting facilities should receive monitoring data and some local benefit rather than only trucks and unpleasant smells. Planning should prevent environmental facilities from being concentrated in politically weaker communities.

Binding safeguards would make that choice more comfortable.

Part 3 · 15 questions

PART 3 · 1

Why is water scarcity becoming a major urban issue?

Suggested phrasal verbs
use upbring up to scale
Model answer · 128 words

Cities can use up nearby rivers, reservoirs and aquifers as populations grow and rainfall becomes less reliable. Climate change increases drought risk, while ageing networks waste treated water before it reaches customers. Governments must bring leakage reduction, reuse and catchment protection up to scale rather than depending on one new reservoir. Demand also matters: gardens, industry and cooling compete with household use. Water scarcity becomes an urban governance problem when supply, planning and pricing are managed separately. Long-term policy should diversify sources while guaranteeing affordable basic access. New development should also demonstrate where its future water will come from instead of assuming supply will appear after planning approval. Water-sensitive urban design can reduce demand and recharge local sources, but it requires coordination across planning, drainage and public space.

PART 3 · 2

Should water prices be increased to reduce demand?

Suggested phrasal verbs
feed costs throughdial back
Model answer · 125 words

Higher prices can encourage households and firms to dial back waste, but tariff design has serious distributional effects. Utilities should not simply feed every infrastructure cost through uniform increases. A basic quantity of drinking water should remain affordable, while excessive or discretionary use can face higher rates. Smart meters and leak assistance help users respond. Prices also fail when major losses occur in the network. Tariff reform is defensible only when institutions publish costs, protect vulnerable households and demonstrate that revenue funds improved service rather than chronic inefficiency. Tariffs can include seasonal or block pricing, but users need accurate meters and a route to challenge incorrect bills. Industrial tariffs should reflect treatment needs and scarcity without encouraging firms to relocate pollution to less regulated regions.

PART 3 · 3

Can wastewater become a reliable water source?

Suggested phrasal verbs
turn intobring up to scale
Model answer · 121 words

Advanced treatment can turn used water into a reliable source for industry, irrigation and, in some systems, drinking supply. Cities should bring water reuse up to scale where scarcity justifies the investment. The main requirements are treatment performance, energy supply, monitoring and public trust. Industrial reuse can protect drinking water without entering household taps, while potable reuse needs stronger communication and multiple safety barriers. Wastewater is relatively predictable because cities produce it continuously. However, reuse should complement leakage repair and source protection rather than legitimise unlimited consumption. Long-term purchase agreements with industry can make infrastructure finance more predictable while preserving household supply. Reuse standards should specify suitable applications so high-quality drinking water is not used where lower-grade recycled water is sufficient.

PART 3 · 4

Is desalination a good solution to water shortages?

Suggested phrasal verbs
design infeed costs through
Model answer · 119 words

Desalination can provide drought-resistant supply for coastal cities, but it has high energy intensity and creates concentrated brine. Projects should design in renewable energy, marine monitoring and responsible brine disposal. Governments must also decide how utilities feed those costs through tariffs. Desalination is valuable when alternatives are insufficient, yet it should not be the first response to leaking pipes or inefficient demand. A diversified system may combine conservation, reuse, reservoirs and desalination. The technology solves physical scarcity more easily than affordability or environmental governance. Brine and emissions should be assessed through lifecycle analysis rather than hidden behind the phrase drought-proof supply. Desalination contracts should disclose energy and environmental costs over the full operating life, not only the construction price.

PART 3 · 5

Why do recycling rates remain low?

Suggested phrasal verbs
sort outdispose of
Model answer · 125 words

Recycling systems fail when products are difficult to separate, rules vary and markets for recovered materials are weak. Households cannot sort out complex packaging that combines several materials, so much of it is disposed of or contaminates collection. Cheap virgin materials also undermine demand for secondary inputs. Governments should standardise labels, improve sorting infrastructure and require recycled content. Yet recycling is only one level of the waste hierarchy. Prevention, reuse and repair usually preserve more value than processing disposable products after one use. Deposit systems and producer rules can improve material value by reducing contamination before products reach sorting facilities. Packaging policy should also reduce unnecessary formats before municipalities invest in increasingly elaborate machinery to separate them.

Clear feedback can also improve household sorting decisions.

PART 3 · 6

Should producers pay for the waste their products create?

Suggested phrasal verbs
take backshift away from
Model answer · 123 words

Producers should bear more responsibility because design decisions determine durability, packaging and repair. Firms can be required to take back products or finance collection through producer responsibility schemes. This creates an incentive to shift away from materials that are difficult or dangerous to manage. However, costs may be transferred to consumers, so rules should reward better design rather than simply collect fees. Public registries and independent audits are necessary. The objective is to move waste costs upstream, where products can be redesigned before disposal becomes inevitable. Small producers need proportionate requirements so compliance does not strengthen the market position of the largest firms. International coordination helps when products cross borders, because a producer scheme limited to one country can be avoided through imports.

PART 3 · 7

Can a circular economy reduce dependence on imports?

Suggested phrasal verbs
bring up to scaleturn into
Model answer · 120 words

A circular economy can reduce some import dependence by recovering metals, plastics and nutrients from domestic waste. Countries should bring material recovery up to scale and turn discarded products into reliable secondary materials. This supports supply-chain resilience, especially for materials exposed to geopolitical risk. However, recycling cannot replace all primary extraction because demand grows, materials degrade and collection is incomplete. Circularity is strongest when combined with lower material use, longer product life and repair. It improves security, but complete material self-sufficiency would be unrealistic. Public procurement can create demand for recovered materials and make investment in processing less dependent on volatile commodity prices. Design for disassembly is particularly valuable in electronics, where mixed materials and proprietary parts make recovery expensive.

PART 3 · 8

Is waste incineration compatible with circularity?

Suggested phrasal verbs
use upclose down
Model answer · 119 words

Incineration can reduce landfill volume and generate energy, but it may also use up materials that could have been reused or recycled. Authorities should close down unsafe or highly polluting facilities, yet modern plants can play a limited role for contaminated residual waste. The danger is infrastructure lock-in: long contracts may require a steady waste supply, weakening prevention. Policy should apply the waste hierarchy and avoid counting energy recovery as equivalent to recycling. Incineration is a disposal technology with possible energy benefits, not the centre of a circular economy. Local communities should receive air-quality data and meaningful consultation before long-term waste contracts are signed. Incinerator capacity should not be planned using waste forecasts that assume prevention policies will fail.

PART 3 · 9

How should hazardous waste be managed?

Suggested phrasal verbs
clean upclose down
Model answer · 118 words

Hazardous waste requires tracking, secure treatment and long-term liability. Regulators should close down illegal or unsafe sites and require polluters to clean up contamination. Burying toxic material may appear cheap, but leaks can threaten groundwater for generations. The polluter-pays principle should finance monitoring and remediation, although government may need to intervene when companies disappear. Export rules also matter because wealthy countries should not transfer dangerous materials to places with weaker enforcement. A pollution-prevention principle is justified where failure would be irreversible. Workers handling dangerous material need training, protective equipment and medical monitoring rather than being treated as another disposal cost. Public authorities need emergency funds because abandoned hazardous sites cannot wait through years of litigation before containment begins.

PART 3 · 10

Do individual recycling habits matter?

Suggested phrasal verbs
sort outdial back
Model answer · 122 words

Individual habits matter because clean separation improves material quality and reduces collection costs. People should sort out waste correctly and dial back unnecessary purchases. However, behaviour is constrained by product design, collection systems and local infrastructure. A consumer cannot recycle a multi-layer package when no facility accepts it. Governments and producers therefore carry greater structural responsibility. Behavioural campaigns work best when rules are simple, bins are convenient and feedback shows what happens after collection. Personal effort should support system design, not compensate for its absence. Schools and public buildings can model consistent separation, but infrastructure must remain the same across neighbourhood boundaries. Collection systems must also serve informal settlements and remote communities, where absence of service often leads directly to open dumping.

PART 3 · 11

Should cities ban single-use plastics?

Suggested phrasal verbs
shift away fromput into operation
Model answer · 122 words

Cities should shift away from the most avoidable single-use plastics, especially where reusable alternatives already exist. They should simultaneously put refill, return and washing systems into operation. A ban without infrastructure may replace one disposable material with another that has similar environmental costs. Exemptions are also needed for medical safety and accessibility. Policy should focus on lifecycle impact and product function rather than treating every plastic item identically. The goal is reduced material throughput, not a ceremonial switch from one throwaway object to a slightly heavier one. Reuse targets should measure avoided production, not merely the number of replacement materials sold after a ban. Businesses should report reduction in packaging weight and reuse rates so bans are evaluated through circularity performance indicators.

PART 3 · 12

Can resource recovery make wastewater treatment profitable?

Suggested phrasal verbs
turn intobring up to scale
Model answer · 122 words

Treatment plants can turn sewage sludge into a source of biogas, nutrients and usable biosolids. Resource sales may help pay for operations, although they rarely eliminate the need for public finance. Governments can bring these systems up to scale where recovered products have reliable markets, because resource recovery changes the economics of treatment. Anaerobic digestion can offset energy use, while phosphorus recovery reduces imported fertiliser demand. Profitability depends on quality standards, stable buyers and careful control of contaminants. Contaminant limits and farmer confidence are essential when recovered nutrients are intended for agricultural use. Treatment operators should remain cautious about microplastics, heavy metals and persistent chemicals in recovered products. Regulators should publish whether recovered energy and nutrients genuinely reduce operating costs over time.

PART 3 · 13

How can governments prevent waste rather than manage it?

Suggested phrasal verbs
design intake back
Model answer · 118 words

Governments should design in prevention through product standards, procurement and taxation. Durability, refillability and repairability standards reduce waste before collection. Producer schemes can require companies to take back goods, creating reasons to design them for disassembly. Taxes on virgin materials and disposal can support reuse, while public purchasing creates markets for repaired products. Prevention is politically harder than recycling because it affects business models and consumption volumes. Nevertheless, it is the highest priority in the waste hierarchy and usually preserves the greatest material value. Governments should also remove tax rules that make new products cheaper than repair labour and refurbished alternatives. Repair rights should include software support, because physically durable devices become waste when manufacturers end essential updates.

PART 3 · 14

What makes water reuse socially acceptable?

Suggested phrasal verbs
put into operationdesign in
Model answer · 127 words

Public acceptance depends on trust, not only engineering. Utilities should put pilot projects into operation transparently and design in independent monitoring, emergency procedures and public reporting. People need clear explanations of treatment barriers and comparisons with existing source risks. Consultation should occur before decisions are final, not after authorities have chosen a project. Acceptance also grows when reuse addresses a visible shortage and institutions have a strong service record. Dismissing concern as irrational usually damages the very confidence that potable reuse requires. Independent health authorities are often more credible messengers than the utility promoting the project. Demonstration projects can help, but utilities must explain failures as openly as successful results. Public dashboards can show treatment performance continuously instead of expecting trust to rest on one consultation event.

PART 3 · 15

What would a genuinely circular city look like?

Suggested phrasal verbs
bring up to scaledial back
Model answer · 120 words

A circular city would bring repair, reuse, water recycling and material recovery up to scale while dialling back unnecessary extraction and disposal. Buildings would be adaptable, products designed for maintenance and wastewater treated as a source of water, nutrients and energy. Collection systems would remain universal because informal dumping undermines health and flood resilience. Policy would measure resource productivity rather than only recycling rates. Circularity would also be socially fair: low-income communities would receive reliable services and would not host every landfill, treatment plant or pollution risk. Industrial symbiosis can connect one firm’s by-products with another firm’s inputs, provided transport and quality controls remain practical. Circular planning should also reduce construction waste through modular design and reuse of building components.

10. Five IELTS Writing Task 2 topics

Before writing: check that each body paragraph has a clear topic sentence, explanation, development and a relevant consequence or example. Your position must remain consistent from the introduction to the conclusion.
TASK 2 · 1

Cities facing water scarcity should invest heavily in wastewater reuse, even if the public is initially uncomfortable with the idea. To what extent do you agree or disagree?

Optional collocation bank
water scarcityturn intoindustrial reusepotable reuseput into operationwater efficiencylong-term public valuewater stressfreshwater availability
Model essay · 311 words

Cities facing persistent water scarcity should invest in wastewater reuse, although public confidence must be built through transparent institutions rather than ignored. I therefore agree with major investment where reuse is technically and economically suitable.

The strongest argument is reliability. Wastewater is produced continuously, so advanced treatment can turn a waste stream into a stable resource. Industrial reuse and irrigation can protect drinking-water reserves, while carefully designed potable reuse may diversify urban supply. What reuse offers is a source that grows with the city instead of depending entirely on rainfall.

However, public discomfort is understandable. Treatment failures could affect health, and institutions may have weak records of communication. Authorities should put independent monitoring into operation, publish results and establish emergency procedures. Were residents excluded from decisions, technically safe projects could still lose social legitimacy.

Investment should begin with applications that create obvious benefits, such as industrial demand or park irrigation. This allows utilities to build operational experience. Potable projects require several treatment barriers, operator training and continuous testing. Reuse should not excuse waste. Cities must repair leaks, protect catchments and improve water efficiency. Energy costs also need assessment, because advanced treatment can be intensive. Tariffs should protect basic access while reflecting the cost of new infrastructure. Public support is justified because reuse provides long-term public value and drought resilience beyond individual customers. Long-term purchase agreements can improve finance, particularly where factories commit to buying recycled water. Public contracts must still preserve emergency household supply and prevent one operator from controlling essential infrastructure. Education should use comparisons with ordinary treatment rather than pretending conventional sources are naturally pure. Rivers and groundwater already require protection and treatment.

In conclusion: cities should invest heavily in reuse where scarcity is structural. Public concern should shape safeguards and communication, but it should not prevent a safe source from being developed when conventional supplies are becoming unreliable.

TASK 2 · 2

Some people believe higher water prices are necessary to reduce demand, while others argue that water should remain very cheap because it is essential. Discuss both views and give your opinion.

Optional collocation bank
dial backwater efficiencyleakage reductionfeed costs throughwater stressfreshwater availabilitygroundwater depletionaquifer rechargewater abstraction
Model essay · 315 words

Water is essential for life, yet supplying and treating it requires infrastructure and energy. Some people therefore favour higher prices to reduce demand, while others argue that affordability should dominate. In my view, progressive pricing can encourage efficiency without threatening basic access.

Higher prices make waste more visible. Households may repair leaks and dial back discretionary use, while companies invest in water efficiency. Revenue can support leakage reduction and new treatment. What a price signal can change is behaviour at the margin, especially for gardens, pools and inefficient industrial processes.

The opposing argument is that water is not an ordinary commodity. Uniform tariff increases create harmful distributional effects because low-income families spend a larger share of income on essentials. Utilities may also feed the cost of their own inefficiency through to customers. A household should not face unaffordable bills because a public network loses large volumes through old pipes.

A balanced tariff should provide an affordable basic allowance and charge higher rates for excessive use. Meter accuracy and leak assistance are essential. Not only should customers receive incentives to conserve, but utilities should also face performance targets. Social tariffs can protect vulnerable users, while commercial tariffs reflect scarcity and treatment requirements. Revenue should be transparent and dedicated to service improvement. Public communication matters during reform. Residents need to understand why prices change and how assistance works. Seasonal pricing may be appropriate where summer demand creates scarcity, but households need advance notice. Landlords should also repair leaks inside rental property so tenants are not charged for defects they cannot control. Utility accountability matters because tariff increases are easier to justify when financial accounts and service targets are public.

In conclusion: water should not remain artificially cheap for every use, because this encourages waste and underinvestment. Progressive pricing can reduce demand fairly if basic needs are protected and utilities demonstrate that they are reducing their own losses.

TASK 2 · 3

Desalination plants are becoming more common in drought-prone coastal regions. Do the advantages outweigh the disadvantages?

Optional collocation bank
reverse osmosisdrinking waterenergy intensitybrine disposalwater reusefeed costs throughwater stressfreshwater availabilitygroundwater depletion
Model essay · 305 words

Desalination provides a rainfall-independent source for coastal regions, making it attractive during severe drought. Its advantages can outweigh the disadvantages, but only where energy, cost and marine impacts are managed carefully.

The primary benefit is reliability. Reverse osmosis can produce drinking water when reservoirs and rivers decline. This diversifies supply and reduces the risk that a long drought causes emergency restrictions. What desalination provides is physical security against one specific source of climate variability.

The disadvantages are substantial. Plants have high energy intensity, raising costs and emissions when electricity is fossil-based. Brine disposal may affect marine ecosystems, while intake systems can harm organisms. Large projects also require expensive pipelines and long contracts.

Governments should compare desalination with leakage repair, conservation and water reuse. Only when cheaper measures are insufficient should desalination become the dominant new source. Renewable electricity and efficient membranes can improve performance, but they do not remove every impact. Tariffs create another challenge. Utilities may feed high capital costs through to households, so affordability protections are necessary. Independent environmental monitoring should continue after construction rather than ending with project approval. A diversified water portfolio is safer than dependence on one technology. Desalination can provide a drought reserve while reuse and demand management supply ordinary needs. Emergency planning should identify how quickly plants can increase output and what happens if electricity supply fails. Desalination is drought-resistant but not independent of every infrastructure risk. Environmental permits should include brine dispersion, marine intake and cumulative impacts from several facilities along one coast. Public agencies should publish operating data so future projects are evaluated against real costs rather than optimistic forecasts.

In conclusion: desalination is valuable for water-stressed coastal regions, and its advantages can exceed its disadvantages. It should remain one carefully regulated component of supply rather than an excuse to ignore leaks, demand and ecosystem protection.

TASK 2 · 4

Electronic waste is increasing rapidly around the world. What problems does this cause, and what measures should governments and manufacturers introduce?

Optional collocation bank
dispose ofproducer responsibilitytake backrepairability standardswater stressfreshwater availabilitygroundwater depletionaquifer rechargewater abstraction
Model essay · 307 words

Electronic waste is growing as consumers replace phones, computers and appliances more frequently. This creates health, environmental and resource problems.

Devices contain valuable metals, but they also include hazardous chemicals. Informal workers may burn cables or use acids without protection, exposing themselves and nearby communities. When equipment is disposed of in mixed waste, batteries can cause fires and toxic material may reach soil or water. What is lost is both material value and an opportunity to reduce new extraction.

Governments should require producer responsibility. Manufacturers can take back products, finance safe collection and report recovery. Repairability standards should guarantee spare parts, manuals and software support. Not only must devices be recyclable, but they should also remain usable for longer.

Consumers need convenient return points and trusted refurbishment markets. Public procurement can buy repaired equipment, creating demand and demonstrating quality. Data security procedures are important because people may keep unused devices rather than return them. Export rules should prevent hazardous equipment being labelled as reusable when it is actually waste. Recycling facilities need worker protection and pollution controls. Small informal collectors can be integrated into formal systems rather than simply criminalised. Taxes or fees on short-lived products may support collection, but standards should avoid making essential devices unaffordable. Manufacturers should disclose battery chemistry and component composition so recyclers can handle products safely. Modular design would allow one failed part to be replaced instead of forcing disposal of the entire device. International standards can reduce illegal waste exports and make producers responsible across borders. Retailers and repair businesses can offer trade-in services that make safe return easier than storing obsolete devices indefinitely.

In conclusion: electronic waste wastes critical materials and exposes workers to dangerous substances. Longer product life, manufacturer take-back and safe professional recovery can address the problem more effectively than relying on consumers to find the correct bin.

TASK 2 · 5

Why do many recycling systems fail to create a circular economy? What policies could make material recovery more effective?

Optional collocation bank
circular economysort outproducer responsibilityrepairability standardsrecycled contentmaterial recoveryshift away fromwaste hierarchywater stress
Model essay · 307 words

Many recycling systems collect large quantities of waste yet fail to create a genuinely circular economy. The main reasons are poor product design, contamination and weak demand for recovered materials.

Products often combine plastics, adhesives and components that are difficult to separate. Households then struggle to sort out packaging, while facilities receive contaminated loads. Even successfully recovered material may remain unsold when virgin resources are cheaper. What the system lacks is a connection between collection, design and stable end markets.

Governments should start upstream. Producer responsibility and repairability standards encourage products that last longer and can be disassembled. Deposit systems improve collection quality for packaging. Requirements for recycled content create buyers for secondary materials. Collection rules should be standardised, and municipalities need modern material recovery facilities. Not only should citizens separate waste, but producers should reduce the complexity of what they sell.

Economic instruments can support the transition. Landfill taxes and charges on virgin material change relative prices, while public procurement creates demand for recycled and refurbished goods. Policies must also shift away from products with no realistic recovery route. Measurement should follow the waste hierarchy. A high recycling rate is less impressive if total waste continues rising. Governments should report prevention, reuse and absolute material use alongside recycling. Repair and reuse infrastructure deserves equal attention. Repair cafés, professional refurbishment and spare-parts markets preserve more value than shredding. Tax systems can reduce labour taxes on repair while charging more for virgin resource use. Reliable data is necessary because reported recycling may include exported material whose final treatment is unknown. Regional cooperation can also create larger markets for secondary materials where one city alone cannot support specialised processing.

In conclusion: recycling systems fail when they operate as isolated waste services. Circularity requires durable design, simple collection, reliable processing and markets that value recovered materials before products are discarded.

Course index · Module 1 · Chapter 14

Academic English Studio · Topic 14 · Water Scarcity, Waste and the Circular Economy
